title: Duke-Michigan game canceled over broadcast rights; Texas Tech replaces Wolverines
dek: Duke and Michigan's December 21 nonconference men's basketball game at Madison Square Garden has been canceled due to unresolved broadcast-rights disputes tied to Duke's exclusive Amazon Prime Video deal. Texas Tech will replace Michigan in the same time slot at MSG.
author: The GoCheckMySports Desk
key_fact: A broadcast-rights misinterpretation forced Duke and Michigan to attempt relocation to Miami's LoanDepot Park, but disputes persisted even after the move, ultimately derailing the game entirely.
bottom_line: The Duke-Texas Tech game on December 21 proceeds as scheduled on Prime Video. The sources do not identify which party bears responsibility for the broadcast-rights misinterpretation, leaving the specific contractual failure unresolved; clarification on whether other Amazon-related Duke games face similar risks would test whether this was an isolated error or symptomatic of deeper partnership strain.
body: Duke and Michigan's nonconference men's basketball game, originally scheduled for December 21 at Madison Square Garden, has been canceled after broadcast-rights disputes made the matchup unplayable. According to ESPN's reporting, a misinterpretation of broadcast rights forced Duke and Michigan to attempt relocating the game to LoanDepot Park, home of the Miami Marlins. Even after the attempted relocation, broadcast issues remained unresolved, and all parties could not reach agreement. Texas Tech will now play Duke on December 21 at MSG, also airing exclusively on Prime Video.

The cancellation punctures a high-profile game born from Duke's unprecedented exclusive streaming partnership with Amazon Prime Video, announced in late April. Under the deal, Prime Video will exclusively present three Duke neutral-site nonconference games per season, making it Amazon's first entry into live college sports broadcasting. The platform was set to carry the UConn game in Las Vegas on November 25, the Michigan game on December 21, and Gonzaga in Detroit on February 20. The UConn and Gonzaga games remain scheduled.

Duke worked with the ACC and ESPN to secure the ability to play these three neutral-site games with Amazon, agreeing in exchange to participate in select ESPN-owned and operated neutral-site events during the 2027-28 and 2028-29 seasons. The sources do not specify which party misinterpreted the broadcast rights or how the contractual language created the dispute in the first place.

The Duke-Texas Tech matchup is a rematch of last year's game on December 20, when Texas Tech won 82-81 at the Garden. The canceled Duke-Michigan contest was itself a rematch, following a February meeting in Washington, D.C., won by Duke. According to ESPN's reporting, Duke is ranked No. 2 in ESPN's Way-Too-Early Top 25 after losing consensus Player of the Year Cameron Boozer and second-leading scorer Isaiah Evans. Coach Jon Scheyer has brought in the nation's No. 2-ranked recruiting class and Wisconsin transfer John Blackwell, described as "the best guard in the portal this spring."

Texas Tech enters the matchup on a run of three straight NCAA tournaments under coach Grant McCasland. The program has recorded 74 wins under McCasland's tenure and has not finished lower than third in the Big 12 during his time in Lubbock. Texas Tech's All-America forward JT Toppin suffered a torn ACL in February and is expected to suit up next season.
